data.title: Euroseas: Stronger Coverage, Higher Rates, Same Deep Discount
data.description:
In the past couple of articles I've shared on Euroseas (ESEA), I've been bullish on this quite small containership owner on the grounds that it has continued to trade below a charter-adjusted NAV. Because of that and the fact that the company has been carrying a fleet whose cash flows have been visible far out into the calendar, my thesis has been that there is a safety net and potential for enduring upside.

That was the heart of the thesis in Q2, and it stayed pretty intact in Q3. Today, I am revising the stock after the Q4 report has already been out for a while to support that not only does that very case still stand, but the shape of the story is even cleaner now.

Euroseas closed last year with another very profitable quarter, a dividend hike, deeper forward coverage, and a clean balance sheet, so the fact that the stock remains equally discounted today despite its prolonged rally suggests further gains could very well be sustained. Given the fact that containership leasing rates continue to surge, I believe ESEA stock remains quite compelling today.

Charter Book

Euroseas has been sitting on a strong charter book since the pandemic days, when rates surged and liners were locking up ships for longer durations. It was already well covered when I last looked at the stock. But by Q4, that coverage had gone out even further, to almost 87% for this year and 71% for 2027. And if you look at management's commentary on the following slide in the Q4 presentation, average rates are still moving higher into 2028.

Momentum toward the Q4-end started to pick up, as Euroseas booked Leonidas Z, Gregos, and Terataki on 35- to 37-month charters at $30,000 per day. Then in February, EM Spetses, a 2007-built 1,740 TEU feeder, was extended for 22 to 24 months at $21,500 per day, over $3,000 per day above its existing rate.

With Euroseas' next-12-month cash flow breakeven remaining so manageable at about $12,190 per vessel per day, it's fairly easy to see why the company will keep printing money against charter rates locked in the low-$30,000s on much of the fleet. Rates remain on the rise as well, but I will get to that in a minute.

Fleet Moves

Euroseas' fleet has changed a little since my last article. The company sold the 2005-built Marcos V in October for $50 million, disposing of a 20+-year-old 6,350 TEU vessel. After the sale, the fleet had 15 feeders and 6 intermediates, and the fleet's average age was 13.1 years. Therefore, compared with Q3, the company is a touch smaller on the water today.

The newbuild side is where things get more interesting. At the end of Q4, not a whole lot had changed. Euroseas still had four 4,484 TEU intermediate newbuilds due in 2027 and 2028, and all of them had already been placed on long-term employment. That helps explain why so much of those two years is already booked and why the growth/fleet replacement story looks fairly low-risk at this point.

However, after the quarter, management remained active as they ordered two 2,800 TEU high-reefer containerships for delivery in June and August 2028 for ~$46.35 million each, with options for up to four more. I like that move because, once again, it was quite calculated. Euroseas gets to pursue modern tonnage while maintaining a healthy balance sheet in the face of rising chartering rates.

Rates Stay Hot

I have already mentioned rising rates a couple of times, so here's why current conditions continue to favor Euroseas. Note that freight rates themselves have been more volatile than charter rates. Still, one-year charter rates were near historic highs through Q1 of this year across all classes.

That lines up with the HARPEX Index. Harper Petersen’s April 10 assessments had:

  • 1,700 TEU ships at $17,250/day,
  • 2,500 TEU at $33,000/day ,
  • 4,250 TEU at $44,000/day, and
  • 6,500 TEU at $55,000/day

for 6-to-12-month periods. The broader HARPEX index also sits at a 3+ year high of 2,239, which is obviously great news for Euroseas and the upcoming bookings we will see with respect to the 2026-2027 gaps in the calendar.

The NAV Math Still Works

Back in Q3, I had noted that Euroseas' estimated charter-adjusted NAV was about $84.9 per share. At the time, the stock traded at $61.55, thus at a 30% discount. Given the move up in rates, it makes sense that ESEA’s charter-adjusted vessel value was higher this time, reaching $664.2 million. That put NAV at about $660.9 million, which works out to $93.7 a share.

The stock is trading at $69.15 right now, which implies roughly a 27% discount. That’s narrowed a little from where it was in my mid-December write-up. But with rates having gone up further since February, today’s actual NAV is probably higher too, so the discount may still be closer to 30%+. So yes, the stock has gone up, but it still looks about as cheap as before, and now it also comes with some extra tailwinds like more coverage days and higher rates.

I should mention that there is some risk in the industry on the supply side of the equation, because the overall containership orderbook has climbed to a worrisome ~35% of the fleet in TEU terms. Euroseas acknowledges the possibility of a softer market ahead if Red Sea rerouting fades or freight stays weak. However, allow me to remind you that Euroseas’ exposure is still concentrated in feeders and intermediates. There, supply looks less oppressive than in the giant boxship classes.

Conclusion

Aside from a brief period between early 2024 and mid-2025, when I wrote two neutral takes, I’ve stayed bullish on ESEA since late 2021. It's also fun to note that since my initial November 2021 piece, the stock is up a market-beating 334%.

In more recent quarters, I have held the view that as long as Euroseas keeps extending coverage, harvesting attractive rates, and modernizing the fleet, all while its stock trades far below charter-adjusted NAV, one can take advantage of its rising profits and the potential closing of the valuation gap to make good money on the stock.

Returns continue to compound due to the company winning on the coverage and rates fronts. Still, the valuation gap has yet to close. With rates on the rise set to continue driving strong profits and the NAV gap supporting the case for additional returns while proving a wide margin of safety, I maintain my bullish stance on ESEA.

data.title: New Era Energy & Digital: Still Not Suitable For Investors
data.description:
The last article on New Era Energy & Digital, Inc. (NUAI) mentioned that the company seemed to have a chief existence of raising cash at the current time. Therefore, the latest stock offering that netted the company nearly $100 million should not have come as a surprise. The stock offering itself was for roughly 30 million shares with an overallotment of roughly 4 million more. The prospectus itself used roughly 60 million shares outstanding as the number of shares issued before the offering.

Going Concern Statement

That is quite a bit of dilution for a company with a going concern statement.

Going Concern

Our cash and cash equivalents are not sufficient to fund our planned operations for a period of at least one year from the date these financial statements are issued. Until we can generate substantial revenue and achieve profitability, we will need to raise additional capital to fund our ongoing operations and capital needs. There is no assurance, however, that additional financing will be available when needed or that we will be able to obtain financing on terms acceptable to us. These conditions raise substantial doubt about our ability to continue as a going concern.

This comes from the annual report. To me, that statement above eliminates this idea as an investment consideration for most investors. Consider that a “full stop” and really not go any further. But there is more still.

Litigation

But there is litigation in addition to the going concern statement with the state of New Mexico concerning the company's existing oil & gas business:

Another key idea from the litigation is that this company only recently entered the data center business. The company was in another business, as demonstrated by the litigation above, and that business has yet to be completely closed down.

That makes one wonder exactly how long the data center emphasis will last as a strategy.

As shown above, the company was really formed in fiscal year 2024. More importantly, one has to question why any oil and gas business came along with a data center creation idea. The two are very separate industries. This is especially true since the New Mexico litigation can take time and money (among other resources) away from the proposed business of the company.

SharonAI Interest Acquisition

This particular transaction results in the holders of the SharonAI business making quite a profit at the expense of company shareholders. I do assume that since both companies had a 50% interest, the value of their contributions was about equal.

It turns out that a fair amount of the recent stock offering will pay for the above transaction. I leave it to readers to read the stock prospectus on that part of the deal. That stock offering dilutes current shareholders in the 50% neighborhood.

Very little (relatively speaking) of that share offering is going towards future expenses that help the company achieve its goal of creating data centers (which the last article covered). Instead, much of the stock offering is going towards repaying debt like the convertible note above.

Even that convertible note has a provision for some value to be repaid with shares instead of cash. This makes the next quarterly report a “must-read” for any shareholder or potential investor. The actual balance sheet with a reported cash balance will be extremely important to see, as then we will know the details of this transaction.

No matter what, SharonAI has made quite a profit on this venture that has yet to show any profit at all for the company.

Income Statement

As a general rule, I want a five-year history for a newly public company as constituted before I consider investing. Since this company was really formed in 2024, I would still be watching the action from the sidelines.

As noted above, all the company's revenue comes from the oil & gas business that is under litigation. But even that has lease operating expenses exceeding the revenue. That is hardly helping the cash burn situation for this essentially development-stage company.

If anything, that revenue indicates a lack of focus on the current strategy. It also brings into question the seriousness of the current strategy (compared to a major investor getting out of a tough situation). It will be very interesting to see how the New Mexico litigation unfolds, given the financials shown above.

The impairment expenses of those oil and gas assets are not encouraging either. It would appear that the company will take a sizable loss on acquired assets without having seen material benefits from these assets in its short existence.

Cash Flow Statement

Cash flow indicates a substantial burn rate with a mention of oil and gas.

Respective amounts spent on various expenditures are not given. But again, one has to wonder about the presence of the oil & gas business compared to a stated goal of creating data centers.

Given that much of the cash raised will be spent repaying current debt, any cash raised is likely not to last long unless there is a clear focus on the data center goal, combined with far more detail on a long-term plan, complete with financing.

Credit Facility

Now the company did mention a $290 million credit facility. But rarely is all that money available without “strings attached.” Some conditions are stated in the announcement. However, generally, the actual agreement has more complications than ever fit into an announcement.

Moreover, as to whether or not this gets the company to actual revenue and expenses of a going concern is entirely up for discussion.

For me, there is definitely not enough history for me to even consider such a successful conclusion. I, instead, will get out the popcorn and soda to watch from the sidelines.

Summary

Most new companies “disappear without a trace” within roughly five years. This is covered in just about any finance book that covers new issues. David Dreman touches on this in his “Contrarian Investing” series of books, while Ken Fischer touches on this with his “Super Stocks” series of books. I am sure this gets covered in a lot of other places.

Both authors would tell you this company has about 3 more years to go, and the outlook is not really good. As a result, this stock has to be a very strong sell (as in AVOID big time). There have got to be better choices out there.

Risks

A positive outlook depends upon the satisfactory settlement of the New Mexico litigation, combined with the accomplishment of the data center goals. Currently, that would appear to be a very tall order.

The person taking the job of Chief Operating Officer and President was announced in January 2026. A Chief Financial Officer came a month or so later. There is not a lot of history for the company or the senior officers at this time. Maybe everything will turn out ok in the future despite the short history. But I would say it would be more than reasonable to have doubts about the future given the situation.

The “going concern” statement in the annual report makes this idea a nonstarter for me despite the successful sale of stock. That statement makes the risk level far too high for the average investor. Combined with the payment of debt and the cash burn, I really have a hard time seeing a successful future.

The stock dilution this year signifies a very significant long-term risk in that the company could succeed. But the stock dilution to get there could be substantial enough to eliminate any investment profit.

data.title: What Does The Iran War Mean For Emerging Markets?
data.description:
By Adriaan du Toit, Jingting Deng and Edward Williams

In a volatile period, we believe emerging markets’ stronger fundamentals will shine through the noise.

Conflict in the Middle East—and the resulting energy shock—has raised concerns for emerging markets. Given the breadth of the asset class, we expect implications to vary across countries and issuers, reflecting whether economies are energy exporters or importers, roles in AI supply chains, and supply chain adaptability. Overall, however, our analysis reveals that emerging markets face the current crisis from a position of strength.

Indeed, more credible economic policies, improved governance, lower fixed-income risk premiums and reduced susceptibility to inflation all point to emerging markets’ increasing convergence with developed markets. In addition, emerging countries’ potential growth rates look robust compared with many developed peers.

Yet, in capital markets, a headline-driven, volatile environment is creating greater dispersion across emerging-market countries, sectors and companies. We believe the emerging world’s enormous breadth offers meaningful opportunity to find countries and securities whose valuations have become disconnected from fundamentals.

The US Dollar Remains Central to the Overall Outlook

A weakening US dollar remains central to a positive emerging-market outlook, because emerging countries’ hard-currency debts are mostly denominated in dollars, as are vital commodities.

The initial air strikes on Iran triggered a risk-off market phase, and with it an uptick in the US dollar. But we believe the dollar’s recent weakening trend will resume. The reason? Not only is the currency still expensive relative to history, but structural forces—including concerns about the direction of US policy—continue to drive de-dollarization.

A weaker dollar typically means easier monetary conditions, space for rate cuts in developing countries, and lower risk aversion—all favorable for emerging markets (Display).

Oil Is a Major Driver for Emerging Countries

Higher oil prices were the war’s immediate economic impact. As with most geopolitical events, the market’s initial reaction has been driven by volatility and risk aversion. What ultimately matters for fundamentals, however, is the duration of the conflict rather than the magnitude of the initial price shock. If sustained, continuing higher energy prices are likely to lift headline inflation and weigh on global growth. As a result, financial conditions quickly began to tighten when the conflict began.

Higher energy prices don’t impact every emerging economy through the same channels, but financial markets start by distinguishing between oil importers and oil exporters (Display).

To assess resilience to shocks, it’s also important to assess countries’ respective policy spaces and reserve buffers. Remittances, tourism and proximity to the conflict zone are all important considerations too, in our analysis.

Taking these factors into account, Egypt, Pakistan, Sri Lanka and Kenya appear among the most vulnerable countries, with Ecuador among the best-placed. The larger economies, such as Brazil, South Africa and Mexico, look more resilient. Latin American energy and commodity exporters are also ones to watch.

Technology Contributes to Many Emerging Economies

It’s important to keep in mind other major trends affecting emerging markets—even as the war in Iran draws headlines. Several Asian countries are central to AI supply chains, and many emerging nations globally provide AI-critical commodities, putting them in the lead in the global AI rally (Display).

Beyond semiconductors and memory chips, emerging Asian manufacturers also offer “backdoor,” less-known AI exposures such as power and thermal management and data center components. Not only are these less visible investment opportunities, but they also benefit from higher barriers to entry and longer investment cycles, in our analysis.

Reconfiguration of Supply Chains Makes Security Selection Vital

We expect emerging countries to reconfigure their supply chains to mitigate the impact of the oil shock and its second-order effects.

Accordingly, from a security-selection perspective, it’s vital to identify the winners and losers within the same value chain. We think the big winners will have higher technology standards, offer greater reliability and align geopolitically.

For instance, the disruptions to natural gas supplies have increased global demand for LNG carriers. But competition is limited, as the costs of entering this market are high, standards are technically demanding, and China and Korea dominate the market. Because energy has become a prominent national security concern, some western buyers may feel reluctant to buy from China, making Korean shipyards the winner by default.

In our view, conflict-driven deglobalization need not reduce the opportunity for emerging-market investors. But it will require more selectivity and intensive bottom-up research to identify the companies in the right parts of the value supply chain.

China Remains Central to the Outlook

China’s economy remains crucial for the Asia-Pacific region and for many emerging countries worldwide, and although China’s property market and domestic consumption remain soft, there are encouraging signs for emerging-market trading partners.

Chinese policymakers have recommitted to keeping real GDP growth between 4.5% and 5%. We also expect them to help ease some of the disinflationary impulses in the economy.

We see several structural positives for China’s securities markets: the government’s anti-involution measures to reduce overcapacity; improving corporate governance (evidenced by more dividends and buybacks); improved capital discipline resulting in negative net equity issuance; and China’s strong position in critical technologies and commodities, especially those linked to electrification.

We’re already seeing more attractive opportunities in insurance and financial companies because the shareholder-friendly policy shift has led to stronger balance sheets in these sectors.

Stay Alert to Risks—and Rebounds

Rather than treating emerging markets as a single macro trade, we think investors should consider allocating selectively across regions and countries and tilting tactically between emerging equity and debt. They can also use multiple security selection techniques to focus on companies with resilient earnings, pricing power and strategic relevance.

Periods of heightened volatility have historically created opportunities. Our research indicates that, following periods of high volatility where valuations become disconnected from fundamentals, emerging markets have bounced back more strongly than their developed counterparts. We favor building positions in well-placed emerging countries and companies ahead of a normalization in volatility and sentiment.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

MSCI makes no express or implied warranties or representations, and shall have no liability whatsoever with respect to any MSCI data contained herein. The MSCI data may not be further redistributed or used as a basis for other indices or any securities or financial products. This report is not approved, reviewed or produced by MSCI.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Nintendo: The Upward Game Should Start Soon
data.description:
Five months after my previous coverage, Nintendo Co., Ltd. (NTDOY) has already dropped by about 40% despite my positive view. I can’t blame the market for this sharp and hasty reaction, and I think this is part of NTDOY’s price cycle. Even I have started to become more conservative by downgrading my rating from strong buy to buy after the stock rose by 50%, from $14.7, my very first analysis in December 2024, to $22 in November 2025. And I admit I failed to note the more important aspects of its fundamentals, which could have prompted me to continue my downgrade to hold. Now, NTDOY is at its one-year low. Even so, new buying opportunities have already opened at this level. Valuation is already cheap, while momentum shows tendencies to improve due to overselling. These are all supported by its robust fundamentals and new growth drivers.

Nintendo: Market Appeal Is Still Strong

Nintendo Co. Ltd. has capitalized on its enticing games and unique consoles, which helped it secure a solid customer base for three or four decades. But since the 2000s, it has learned to adapt to new technologies and even took advantage of them to maintain a strong market position and huge following. And from games, merchandise, and royalties, it extended to movies, videos, and smart-device content, showing its continued evolution in the midst of competition in a fast-paced environment. Just when you thought it had already reached its summit, Nintendo proved it could still go higher. This was evident in its most recent performance.

For the three quarters of FY 2026, its net sales reached ¥1.906T, up by 99.3% YoY from ¥956.22B. In Q3 2026 alone, it generated ¥806.318B, which was already close to the three-quarter sales in FY 2025. This proves the strong sales or demand for Switch 2. As of its most recent report, it has already sold a total of 17.37M units of Switch 2, which was an increase of 7M units, or 67.66%, in a single quarter. This was also the highest number of units sold since it only sold 5.82M units in Q1 2026 and 4.54 units in Q2 2026. I think this was impressive considering stubborn inflation and new tariffs. Geographically, the Americas remained the core growth driver as it comprised over 40% of the global sales. Europe came second with 24.7%. This can tell us that Nintendo’s global reach remained vast as customer loyalty strengthened.

To have a better view of its sales, we can make an assumption of its pricing and sales. As we all know, the base selling price of each Switch 2 is ¥49,980. If we multiply it by the total number of units sold for three quarters, it already generated ¥868.16B. But of course, the price I used is for Japanese-language-only models. To be more precise, I assumed that it sold multi-language models in other regions. With that, the estimated global sales of Switch 2 from Q1 to Q3 2026 reached $1.090T.

Likewise, costs and expenses rose dramatically. Their combined increase of 127% completely offset revenue growth. But of course, it is like comparing apples to oranges because NTDOY hasn’t released Switch 2 yet in the comparative period. We also have to account for the impact of stubborn inflation and higher tariffs, which made production and shipment of materials and products costlier. The good thing is that its sales remained high enough to cover all operating expenses. In the end, its operating income remained a double-digit value at 15.76%.

Risks

Stubborn inflation is still a primary consideration for Nintendo. Although it has already slowed down, it remains above the target of many central banks. For instance, US inflation is still above the 2.0% Fed target, which means that it still hasn’t been totally controlled yet. Positive inflation means prices are still increasing, which may affect purchasing power, discretionary spending, and margins of the company.

This may be exacerbated by new tariffs because exporting parts, materials, and products to the US may become more expensive. This may increase its cost pressures and even the selling prices. Another thing to consider is the Middle East tension. Although NTDOY has a well-diversified geographical presence and is not heavily reliant on oil or fuel, it may still experience the negative spillovers. For instance, higher oil prices may raise its fuel expenses. And because energy commodities affect large industries like shipping, logistics, manufacturing, and industrial, the economy as a whole may be affected. With fueled inflation, it may also have to raise wages so employees can cope with inflation. And because this is not an essential good with typically a strict suggested retail price, it may be difficult for it to raise prices. It may also prioritize volumes and customer loyalty since it also has to consider competition with other brands like Sony and Xbox.

Mitigants/Drivers

Despite these potential risks, I believe that NTDOY has several strengths to protect its operations and support a rebound.

One aspect to consider is its solid customer base. In gaming, loyalty and following are not measured or determined the same way we do for typical merchandise or services. It took it decades to maintain millions of loyal gamers to sustain its growth and expansion. Its demand is somewhat elastic since it has substitutes like the gaming brands I’ve mentioned earlier. However, product differentiation is what dictates the demand and loyalty for each player. After all, you cannot play Zelda on PlayStation and Xbox. Many games are part of Nintendo exclusives that cannot be played on these consoles. The same applies to PS and Xbox. In addition, product/game compatibility in Switch and Switch 2 ensures that it will generate revenues if gamers can’t purchase the latter. That is why it was still able to sell 3.25M units of Switch hardware and 108.93M units of Switch software.

The strong popularity of Pokémon is another growth driver for NTDOY. In a recent report, Pokémon Pokopia sales for the Switch 2 have already surpassed 2.2M units just four days after its launch on March 5, 2026. This shows that NTDOY capitalizes not only on its consoles but also on its games. I believe this was effective because Pokémon is well-loved for many reasons. One is its anime franchise. Let’s be honest. I bet no anime lover and gamer doesn’t know anything about characters like Pikachu, Charizard, Snorlax, Meowth, and Squirtle. Since the late 1990s or early 2000s, Pokémon has already captured our hearts. So, it won’t be surprising to see more sales.

The surprising success of The Super Mario Galaxy Movie is another driver. As of yesterday, the movie has already grossed over $600M since its release in theaters. With its strong ticket sales, it is expected to get close to $1B in the next few weeks. This proves its appeal across all generations. Indeed, it is not only for individual gamers but for their family as a whole. This ensures NTDOY's popularity across the globe today.

Another core strength of Nintendo is its robust liquidity. Its cash and cash equivalents comprise nearly half of the total assets, proving that it has ample liquid assets. The amount is also more than enough to cover all liabilities. Additionally, most of its equity is retained earnings, which proves that NTDOY has been generating enough earnings to cover business operations without resorting to borrowings. Hence, NTDOY may sustain its capacity, dividends, and innovation.

Valuation

To assess its valuation, I assessed its price ratios. Using P/B, the stock trades at 0.80x its book value, which can be considered reasonable. It is also lower than the five-year average of 0.93x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP, or target price, will be $15.19, or a 17% upside potential. The same applies to the P/S ratio using sales per share and P/CPS using cash per share, as they lead to TPs of $17.25 and $16.35, respectively. The proximity of their TPs proves that NTDOY is already reasonably cheap with some upside at this level.

More interestingly, we can use the forecasted sales of NTDOY to value P/S in a more forward-looking approach. If we convert the forecasted sales of $2.250T, the USD value will be $14.09B. After dividing it by the total shares of 1,164.3M, the sales per share will be $12.10, while the P/S FWD will be 1.07x ($13.00/$12.10). This is still lower than the average of 1.49x. Using this, the P/S FWD TP is slightly higher at $17.99, proving the cheapness of the stock.

I also made updates on its DCF Model. Because the sales forecast remains the same, I also left its FCFF untouched. But I still updated its cash and cash equivalents based on its most recent report. I still set its perpetual growth rate at 2.0% to be in line with my previous coverage. However, there were notable changes in its WACC. I increased its risk premium from 5.0% in my previous coverage to 6.0% to price in more risks. Lastly, my new estimated Beta is 0.28, which means that NTDOY has become much less volatile than in previous coverage, with pricings much more stable than the S&P 500. With all these numbers, I estimated a TP of ¥17,070, or a 105% upside from its current price of ¥8,297. If we translate this upside potential to its USD value, the TP will be $26.70, which is double the current price.

Technicals

NTDOY’s technicals stay weak as its price moves with lower highs and lows. The stock price is also below the 50-day and 200-day SMA, which shows that the current momentum is still weaker than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. The 50-day SMA is still trending downwards, which means that recent market sentiments have been pessimistic. Yet, we can see that the downtrend has flattened a bit. This may suggest that buyers are entering again, while fewer sellers are accepting price bids below the current price.

It is also worth noting the opposite trend of MACD and RSI. Note the decreasing price lows from December 22 to February 25 to April 10. However, the lows of MACD and RSI indicators on the same dates increased continuously. This can tell us that fewer sellers are influencing the market, while the number of buyers increased as the stock became much cheaper. This can be an early bullish divergence signal. More importantly, the stock is almost oversold now, which may prompt buying volume and drive recovery attempts.

Conclusion

Nintendo Co., Ltd. remains a robust company with its solid customer base, supported by its Switch 2 and Pokémon Pokopia. Its robust liquidity and continued innovation help ensure its strong adaptability and competitiveness while sustaining dividends. In addition, its valuation is already cheap after the selloff. Technicals are still weak today, but early bullish divergence calls after the recent overselling may drive some recovery attempts. I’m reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ServiceNow: Cheap Enough To Avoid
data.description:
Today, it's finally a green day in the software industry, and stocks like ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW) are up mid-single digits.

Despite the investor excitement in the software space, I am not joining this rally.

Believe it or not, I am not a bear on ServiceNow. Despite the UBS downgrade last week and the fears of AI disruption, I strongly believe the bull case is not broken.

What I think is broken is the lens that the market is using to value this stock. Based on the nearly 10% drop after a beat and raise in Q4 2025, I believe I was right with my earlier view to remain on the sidelines until the storm comes to an end.

As I discuss in this piece, I don't see any signs of a turnaround. In my view, fundamentals alone are unlikely to lift this stock, and I remain disappointed with the timing of the M&A spree, given what's going on in software after the announcement of Anthropic's Mythos.

Therefore, I believe today's pop is merely due to the beta with the software industry. Just look at the 1-day return heatmap of this industry, with pretty much every constituent in the green:

I don't see enough alpha in ServiceNow to warrant an upgrade. Yes, at 20x forward earnings, this is a cheap stock. However, I am not sure that the multiple compression is a compliment to begin with.

Needless to say, I remain on the sidelines.

The M&A Spree

In my view, the timing for this acquisition spree couldn't have been worse, given the pessimism in the software sector due to AI disruption fears.

Inorganic growth is the last thing you want to hear when the market is worried about the organic growth in this sector.

Let's start with Moveworks.

The company announced this acquisition back in March 2025 for $2.85 billion. In plain English, this acquisition was intended to help employees get support and get routine work done inside the company. Think IT tickets for access, finding company information, resetting passwords, or handling HR questions.

Well, it turns out that the acquisition closed on December 15, 2025, just a few weeks after fears of AI disruption started following Anthropic’s release of Claude Opus 4.5, one of the best models back then for coding and agentic work.

Now, given how late in Q4 the acquisition was done, I wasn't surprised to see these comments from CEO William McDermott during the last earnings call (emphasis added):

Overall, Q4 NNACV growth accelerated both quarter-over-quarter and year-over-year in Q4. Our subscription revenue growth was 21%, 19.5% in constant currency, 1.5 points above the high end of our guidance. Contribution from Moveworks was de minimis.

Looking at the guidance for Q1 and FY 2026, management disclosed the Moveworks contribution.

I'm glad they did, as it shows minimal effect on total growth.

For Q1 2026, the subscription revenue guidance was 18.5%-19% in constant currency. Well, that guidance includes only 100 basis points from Moveworks. In fact, the FY 2026 subscription revenue guidance (19.5%-20% in cc) is also assuming only 100 bps from Moveworks.

The problem that I see comes from the other two acquisitions announced at the end of last year.

Let me start with the main one: Armis.

It was announced back in December last year for $7.75B in cash. It appears that management expects to complete this acquisition in the second half of 2026. Therefore, once closed, the company will have to reassess organic vs. inorganic growth. I see two main issues.

First, given the size of this acquisition (2.7x the value of the Moveworks acquisition), it is likely that its contribution to growth will be significantly higher than the 100 bps of Moveworks. Therefore, the fears of inorganic growth are likely to resume in H2.

Second, Armis is a cybersecurity company. You may already know where the sentiment in this industry is after Anthropic's announcement of Mythos. Otherwise, take a look at the chart below, showing the performance of BUG, a global ETF on cybersecurity.

If that wasn't enough, ServiceNow announced back in December last year a third acquisition. This is a smaller acquisition of a little over $1B for a company that offers an identity security platform (Veza). This acquisition closed in March this year, so we may find out the contribution to growth as soon as the Q1 earnings call. At least, that's what I would expect, given how transparent management was with Moveworks.

ServiceNow Bull Case Still Intact

As I suggested in my last article, fundamentals (or seat growth) aren't deteriorating. Aside from the M&A spree that I discussed above, it's difficult to find any other solid argument against the bull case.

As seen below, the Street expects revenue in the 20s percent for the next 4 quarters.

Despite the recent analyst downgrades, the consensus for earnings has barely changed. In other words, the Street believes the AI disruption fears are just that: fears.

Speaking of downgrades, the stock was down almost 7% last Friday after UBS downgraded ServiceNow to Neutral from Buy and cut its price target to $100 from $170. The same analyst lowered its exit-2026 cRPO growth estimate to 16% constant currency from 20%.

For reference, management guided a cRPO of 20% (cc) for Q1 2026. As I said earlier, the Q1 cRPO guide includes about 100 basis points from Moveworks. So, the organic Q1 growth was about 19%.

Other price target cuts this month included Stifel ($135 from $180), citing a weak U.S. federal spending environment, and BTIG ($185 from $200). Interestingly, analysts here on Seeking Alpha feel quite bullish about this stock, as noted by the recent articles. In fact, as I point out below (red arrow), there was a clear inflection point between hold and buy ratings on this stock, right when the AI disruption fears began.

Are these analysts right?

Give it time, and I believe the answer may be yes. However, I don't see why I should park my capital in a stock that is constantly pressured by headlines and Wall Street analyst downgrades.

This external pressure is compressing a multiple that already looks quite attractive. As I'm writing, the stock is trading at just 20x next year's earnings. When compared to the last 12-month P/E of 50x, it seems like a bargain, in my view.

Looking Ahead

I reiterate my hold on ServiceNow, as I am not convinced that an attractive valuation multiple is enough to turn around the sentiment surrounding this stock.

As seen below, the stock broke a key long-term trend last week, as the fears of Anthropic's Mythos model permeated the industry.

The next technical support seems to be $70. That would be another double-digit drop from today's level. Given the M&A spree and the recent analyst downgrades, I don't see why strong fundamentals in Q1 will turn around this trend. Fundamentals certainly didn't help in Q4, after the company beat the analysts' expectations for subscription revenue in 2026 ($15.53B-$15.57B vs. the Street's estimate at $15.21B).

Overall, I am staying on the sidelines on this one.

data.title: Intel Could Be The Next Micron
data.description:
Thesis Summary

A couple of weeks ago, I wrote about how Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) could be the new Intel Corporation (INTC), comparing the similarities between these two back in the 1990s, when Intel dominated hardware.

Today, I present yet another controversial idea: Could Intel be the next Micron?

While Intel is seen by many as a bloated tech company that has missed the AI revolution, the stock is now making new highs and leading the semiconductors.

Micron’s story is all about capturing the memory bottleneck. But where’s the next bottleneck?

I argue below that Intel could re-emerge as the key player of a different, and potentially more strategic, bottleneck.

The market is slowly waking up to this possibility, but it’s not too late to invest in Intel today.

How Bottlenecks Shift In Every Cycle

Back in the 1990s, as I talked about in my Micron article, CPUs were the bottleneck. It was the dawn of the personal computer, and that was the way to access the internet.

Fast forward to the 2010s, and everyone has a computer. Now it’s all about software and cloud infrastructure. We saw big gains for cloud providers and also software companies with the right talent.

Now, AI has changed everything once again. Software has become commoditised, and will likely never be seen in the same light by the market.

But the era of AI created a shortage in GPUs, which originally favoured NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA).

As AI clusters scaled and we moved from training to inference, memory bandwidth became the next constraint, making Micron one of the hottest stocks in the market.

We’ve also seen big rallies in photonics and connectivity stocks recently

Yes, there will be shortages of all these components for the next two years, but that’s already priced in. We need to look at what comes next.

Intel Today: Broken Or Misunderstood?

This is a question I myself have grappled with.

In my last Intel article, I discussed all the problems with Intel, giving it a Sell rating. But the stock is up 30% since then, so clearly I was wrong.

Intel has made a lot of mistakes over the years. It let Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) take its share in CPUs. It missed the mobile phone revolution, and it became a tech giant too set in its ways.

But perhaps, finally, Intel has woken up.

Intel is now one of the only Western companies investing in semiconductors, at least at a meaningful scale.

Intel is undergoing the painful and expensive process of building fabs, and expanding capacity. However, in doing so, it is positioning itself as a foundry, not just for the US, but also for external customers.

This way takes some time to play out, but by the time it's obvious, like Micron today, the money will be made.

Manufacturing is where the next bottleneck lies.

The Bottleneck Intel Could Control

Already, there’s clear evidence that AI demand is overwhelming production. Reportedly, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) is reaching its limits in terms of capacity.

Meanwhile, money is pouring into the industry, with Japan reportedly investing $4B to support local chipmakers.

Chip production and certain particular areas around the process are the next obvious, yet not so obvious, bottleneck.

Take advanced packaging, for example/

As AI systems scale, more components are put into a single rack or chip.

These integrated systems combine multiple dies, memory stacks, and interconnects, and this dramatically increases complexity.

Advanced packaging has specifically been acknowledged recently as a potential bottleneck.

If Intel can execute here, through technologies like Foveros and EMIB, it will position itself at a critical junction of the AI stack.

It’s no good having all the pieces if you can’t put them together, and that’s an area in which Intel could actually shine.

And of course, let’s not forget the geopolitical dimension here.

AI has the potential to control everything in the economy, including critical infrastructure, utilities, and defense systems.

As such, it’s not just enough to have the technology; you have to be able to produce it.

That’s why governments and corporations alike are looking for alternatives to TSM, and Intel’s push into foundry services aligns directly with this shift.

Valuation

Even though Intel has had a good run, it is not being priced as a future bottleneck.

Intel is being priced like a turnaround story, but a mild one at that. EPS is expected to slowly move from negative to positive territory, with revenue growing in the single digits over the next couple of years.

I think this misses the bigger point.

If Intel is going to sit at the forefront of a new bottleneck, then margins could have huge upside.

Look at TSM, for example, which has increased its gross margin to over 60%.

This means Intel has room to double margins from here, and EPS could easily surprise to the upside as this happens.

I'm talking about a transformation equivalent to that of Micron, which went from tight margins to over 60% margins and more than 6x earnings in a year.

No one saw it coming, though now it seems obvious, and Intel now has the potential to do the same.

Risks

The most obvious risk is that Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company will continue to dominate the market.

The truth is, it hasn’t really been challenged before, but it’s hard to see Intel displacing TSM overnight.

Intel has some guarantee of customers, since the US has an interest in having its own production, but it ultimately has to attract external customers to its foundry business to create real upside.

Of course, Intel is spending heavily, and if returns do not materialise, this will continue to destroy shareholder value rather than create it.

Lastly, with a lot of other countries investing in fab capacity, perhaps the bottleneck will be addressed before Intel can really reap the benefits.

Final Thoughts

Ultimately, I think Intel has a lot of potential upside here, while the downside is limited. Intel is addressing perhaps the most important part of the AI supply chain and trying to create a real alternative.

If it succeeds, we’re looking at a completely transformed business, with huge earnings potential.

data.title: Marqeta: Slowing Growth And Block Dependency Keep Valuation In Check
data.description:
Introduction

Marqeta, Inc. (MQ) is a modern payment card issuing platform, providing cloud services-based infrastructure via APIs. Its business model is focused on transaction processing fees, which directly correlate to the total volume of payments that have passed through the platform, which reached $383 billion in 2025.

The main company‘s problem remains revenue concentration risk, because Block, Inc generates around 44% of all the company‘s revenues, which creates constant pressure in negotiations on service rates. Even though yearly revenues in 2025 grew 23%, reaching $625 million, gross margin stabilized at 70%.

Currently, the company‘s P/S ratio is at 2.8, which reflects the market‘s reorientation from earlier high growth expectations to a more realistic profitability valuation. The company in 2025 recorded a $14 million GAAP net loss, though the management is forecasting that in 2026, there will be a breaking point and it will reach around $10 million in net profits. Such a financial structure shows that economies of scale are starting to offset operating costs, though in 2026, the growth forecast is moderate at around 12-14% due to Block's transition to lower pricing and diversification of Cash App card issuance. Investors value the company as a critical part in fintech infrastructure, though the decreasing take rate ratio and the dependency on one massive client remain the main factors that are limiting share valuation multiple expansion.

Business overview

Marqeta operates as a modern card issuer and payment processing infrastructure provider, its business model is based on commission fees for the volume of transactions processed. In 2025, the company reached a record $383 billion yearly operational value, which marked a 31% growth compared to 2024.

Despite the reached volumes, in 2026, the company is forecasting a moderate revenue growth for this fiscal year. The main thing that is slowing the company down is the Block's change in contract pricing level and two other large client contract renewals, which in 2026 will artificially reduce gross profit growth by around 7%.

Trying to soften this change, Marqeta is managing a purposeful product rotation, increasing higher margin services and loan solutions, especially the weight of buy now pay later segments in the portfolio. At the end of 2025, value-added services were already generating 7% of the gross profit, and loan service volumes grew almost 60% in a year. This change is necessary to maintain a higher revenue collection rate.

Even though the market‘s valuation remains conservative with a 2.8 price to sales ratio, 2026 should be the breaking point where it‘s going to reach a positive net income by the general accounting principles, estimated at $10 million. Diversification in Europe, where volumes are growing two times faster than the overall portfolio, helps consistently lower the dependency on Block, whose concentration in revenues decreased 44%.

Valuation

Marqeta‘s valuation as of today reflects the market‘s skepticism due to the slowing growth and a high revenue concentration risk. The company‘s price-to-sales ratio is currently reaching 2.8, which is lower than the five-year historical average of around 7x, suggesting that investors no longer credit the company's status as a high-growth technology company.

Even though the revenues in 2025 grew 23% to $625 million, the 2026 official forecast is showing a drop to 13% growth, because large clients, especially Block contract renewals, are decreasing the net margin and the potential for generating revenues. Compared to the sector‘s leaders, Marqeta is valued way lower than Adyen N.V. (ADYEY), whose P/S ratio is reaching around 11, due to the high EBITDA margin, while Marqeta in 2025 only fixed a 18% adjusted EBITDA margin.

Competitive pressure from Stripe Issuing and SOFI forces the company to reduce service prices, therefore, the balance with $1.2 billion cash, which makes up around 35% of the market‘s capitalization, gives financial stability, though the 2.2 P/B ratio shows that the market isn‘t confident in a rapid achievement of GAAP profitability. Unlike diversified competitors such as DLocal Limited (DLO) or Affirm Holdings, Inc. (AFRM), Marqeta remains vulnerable due to only one client‘s concentration, which is why the currently the low valuation is a direct outcome of structural risk and the diminishing returns to scale.

Risks

Marqeta‘s business risks in 2026 are focused on a high concentration of income and systematic margin pressure, arising from the contract renewal cycles. The main risks remain the dependency on Block, which at the end of 2025 still generated around 48% of the company‘s revenues.

Even though the contract was extended, new conditions in 2026‘s financial forecast are expected to have a negative impact on gross profit growth due to the mandatory commission cut, while trying to maintain the largest client. Second risk for the company is the aggressive competition with Stripe and Adyen, which are offering vertically integrated payment acceptance and card issuance solutions, thereby decreasing clients need to use a separate Marqeta platform.

This is directly reflected in the net revenue retention, which dropped to 111% compared to 126% in 2024. The company also faces regulatory risks due to the US Federal Reserve Bank‘s proposed Regulation II changes, which could limit debit card interchange fees. Because MQ‘s revenue model is directly linked to part of these fees, any decrease in them directly cuts the net margins, which in 2026 Q1 should be showing signs of stabilization at around the 68-70% range.

Financial risk is the prolonged GAAP loss despite the $1.2 billion cash reserve. In 2025, net loss reached $188 million, and 2026 is supposed to be the breaking point, though it is still uncertain due to high share-based payment costs, which make up around 25% of revenues. Finally, macroeconomic sensitivity to spending in the BNPL sector, which is generating a significant part of TPV, raises the risk to transactional volumes if inflation and interest rates continue to limit middle-class consumption.

Thesis summary

In my view, Marqeta is currently in a transitional phase from being a high-growth story to becoming a more mature, profitability-focused business model. The market clearly overvalued expectations, which is seen in the contraction of the P/S ratio, which no longer reflects a high growth narrative, but rather a more conservative fintech infrastructure‘s valuation. However, this doesn‘t mean that the company lost its strategic value. On the contrary, the company remains a critical link in the modern payments ecosystem, especially in the card issuing segment.

The main question here is not growth, but structure. A high dependency on Block remains the main problem, which limits margin expansion as well as valuation multiple. Even if revenues are growing, the decreasing take rate is showing that the company doesn‘t have full bargaining power, which in the long-term pressures profitability. In my opinion, exactly this aspect explains why the market doesn‘t give the company a higher valuation even while seeing growing TPVs.

On the other hand, I see a clear strategic move in value-added services, and the BNPL segment‘s growth shows an attempt to move away from the commoditized processing model. If this transformation works out, Marqeta could gradually increase margins and lower dependency on one client. Though this is not a quick process, and 2026 will most likely be a proof-of-concept, rather than a full breaking point.

In conclusion, I value Marqeta as an infrastructure play with a clear upside if diversification happens, though the current risk structure, especially client concentration and pricing pressure, limits a bullish valuation.

data.title: The IPO Window Reopens For Small Caps
data.description:
U.S. initial public offerings surged 40 percent in 2025 to 371 listings, with a pipeline of high-profile candidates positioning 2026 as a potential landmark year for the small- and mid-cap ecosystem.

A Market Coming Back to Life

The U.S. IPO market produced 371 listings in 2025, a 40 percent increase from the 266 offerings in 2024, according to MicroVentures. The acceleration marks the third consecutive year of recovery from the post-2021 drought that froze new issuance across much of the venture-backed economy. The pipeline heading into 2026 includes several of the largest private companies in the world, led by Anthropic, OpenAI, SpaceX, and Canva.1

For small-cap investors, the revival of the IPO market matters beyond headline valuations. A functioning primary market brings fresh capital, institutional attention, and liquidity to the asset class. When companies like Kraken (valued at $20 billion) and Dataiku ($3.7 billion) enter the public markets, they expand the investable universe and draw analyst coverage to the smaller end of the capitalization spectrum.2

Private equity firms have been a key driver of the thaw. Firms have been holding investments longer than optimal for their fund cycles, creating what William Fahey of a major insurance provider described as “substantial pent-up demand” for exits. The absence of timely distributions has pressured limited partners. If market conditions remain stable, the exit backlog could fuel sustained issuance through 2026 and into 2027.

U.S. IPO activity has recovered sharply, with 371 listings in 2025, up 40% from 266 in 2024. Source: MicroVentures

Small Caps Lead on Valuation and Earnings

The Russell 2000 gained approximately 12 percent in the first quarter of 2026, dramatically outperforming the S&P 500’s modest 1.5 percent gain. The performance gap reflects a broader rotation away from mega-cap technology and into domestic-oriented companies. Trading volumes in small-cap indices have hit three-year highs as institutional investors rebalance portfolios.3

The valuation case remains compelling. The Russell 2000 trades at approximately 18 times forward earnings versus 26 times for the S&P 500, a 31 percent discount that represents the widest gap in over 25 years. Before the pandemic, small caps typically traded at a premium to large caps. That premium vanished during the mega-cap technology run and has yet to return.4

Earnings projections reinforce the rotation thesis. Bloomberg consensus estimates project Russell 2000 earnings growth of 43 percent over the coming twelve months, compared to 11 percent for the S&P 500. The earnings inflection, following negative growth in 2023 and 2024, has drawn fundamental investors back to the asset class. For IPO candidates evaluating timing, a strong small-cap market raises potential valuations and improves the probability of a successful offering.

The forward P/E discount of small caps vs. large caps has reached its widest level in over 25 years. Source: FactSet

Tailwinds from Policy and Dealmaking

The One Big Beautiful Bill Act, passed in mid-2025, provided meaningful fiscal support for small-cap companies. The restoration of immediate research and development expensing and 100 percent bonus depreciation improved cash flow for capital-intensive manufacturers. The shift of the interest deduction limit from 30 percent of EBIT to 30 percent of EBITDA reduced the tax burden for leveraged smaller companies.5

Merger and acquisition activity is contributing additional momentum. EY-Parthenon projects a 3 percent increase in overall M&A deal volume in 2026, while deal value climbed 36 percent in the first nine months of 2025. Deloitte’s survey of dealmakers indicates that the “vast majority” expect more and bigger deals in 2026. For small caps, M&A activity can drive re-rating in stock prices and create acquisition premiums across entire sectors.6

The combination of a recovering IPO market, historically wide valuation discounts, accelerating earnings growth, and policy tailwinds creates a favorable environment for small-cap investing. The risks remain real: floating-rate debt exposure, the maturity wall, and geopolitical uncertainty all bear watching. The opportunity, however, is increasingly difficult for institutional allocators to ignore. Capital is flowing back to the smallest segment of the public equity market.

Russell 2000 earnings growth is projected at 43% for the next 12 months, compared to 11% for the S&P 500. Source: Bloomberg

Footnotes

1. MicroVentures, 2026 IPO Outlook, December 2025.

2. U.S. News, 8 Best Upcoming IPOs in 2026, updated April 2026.

3. S&P Dow Jones Indices, Russell 2000 factsheet, March 2026.

4. FactSet Earnings Insight, Forward P/E Estimates, March 2026.

5. Chronicle Journal, The Great Rotation: Russell 2000 Outshines Mega-Caps, March 24, 2026.

6. EY-Parthenon, Deal Barometer, January 2026.

Disclosure

This information is not an offer to sell, or solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product, security, or services offered by Jay Hatfield, or Infrastructure Capital Advisors, LLC, ("CA") or its affiliates. ICA, will only conduct such solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product or service offered by ICA, if at all, by (1) purported definitive documentation (which will include disclosures relating to investment objective, policies, risk factors, fees, tax implications and relevant qualifications), (2) to qualified participants, if applicable, and (3) only in those jurisdictions where permitted by law. Jay Hatfield or ICA may have a beneficial long or short position in securities discussed either through stock ownership, options, or other derivatives; nonetheless, under no circumstances does any article or interview represent a recommendation to buy or sell these securities. This discussion is intended to provide insight into stocks and the market for entertainment and information purposes only and is not a solicitation of any kind. ICA buys and sells securities on behalf of its fund investors and may do so, before and after any particular article herein is published, with respect to the securities discussed in any article posted. ICA’s appraisal of a company (price target) is only one factor that affects its decision whether to buy or sell shares in that company. Other factors might include, but are not limited to, the presence of mandatory limits on individual positions, decisions regarding portfolio exposures, and general market conditions and liquidity needs. As such, there may not always be consistency between the views expressed here and ICA’s trading or holdings on behalf of its fund investors. There may be conflicts between the content posted or discussed and the interests of ICA. Please reach out to the ICA for more information. Investors should make their own decisions regarding any investments mentioned, and the information herein should not be relied upon for that purpose. Nothing in this article should be construed as a solicitation or offer, or recommendation, to buy or sell any security. Content in this message is for informational purpose only. None of the views expressed herein constitute financial, investment, tax, or legal advice. The information in this article is believed to be accurate as of the date it is written. Infrastructure Capital Advisors, its directors, officers, employees, and affiliates do not assume any liability for losses that may result from the reliance by any person upon any information or opinions contained herein. Past performance is not indicative of future results. The information contained herein represents our subjective belief and opinions and should not be construed as investment, tax, legal, or financial advice.

Original Source: Infrastructure Capital Advisors

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Sibanye Stillwater: The South Africa Discount
data.description:
Sibanye Stillwater Limited (SBSW) is a major PGM and gold miner based in South Africa. On multiple valuation metrics, SBSW sounds cheap. However, SBSW is being discounted for a reason. More than 80% of its operations are concentrated in one geography—South Africa. Jurisdictional risks associated with this particular geography overwhelmingly drive SBSW's business and bottom line. While management is actively diversifying, it will take time, and even then, it may not be possible to divest more than 50% of their business from South Africa. That indicates that the discounted valuation is less of an opportunity and more of a risk assessment. Therefore, I rate SBSW a Sell/Avoid and may change my rating if there is meaningful geographical diversification.

Business and Geography

Sibanye Stillwater Limited is a diversified miner with the most exposure to platinum group metals (PGMs) and gold. They have a number of deep-level underground mines as well as surface operations. They mainly operate in South Africa, with a smaller footprint in the United States and Europe.

For FY2024, SBSW generated approximately ZAR 112 billion (~$6.7 billion) in revenue. Here's a breakdown from 2024, where revenue breakdown by segment is available:

In 2024, PGM accounted for ~53% of revenue, followed by gold at ~33%. In 2025, we can derive total PGM from geographical breakdown values published, which tells us that PGM was slightly higher at ~56% of total EBITDA. If you include recycling, that goes up to ~67%. Gold remained at 33% of EBITDA. PGM and gold together account for the bulk of the revenue.

As for geographic footprint, in 2024, revenue-based calculations show 73% of operations were concentrated in South Africa alone. In 2025, EBITDA-based numbers show this is now 77%. While this may not be an exact apples-to-apples comparison, it is not wrong to say that South African operations consist of a disproportionately large part of the company. Core mining assets like PGM and gold are mostly concentrated in SA. Here are two tables:

South Africa Contribution—PGM vs. Gold

SA PGM/Gold and total PGM/Gold

The data makes this clear. South Africa accounts for three-quarters of total earnings, with no meaningful change visible between 2024 and 2025. More critically, the core business—PGM and gold—is overwhelmingly exposed to that one geography.

Within PGM, ~80% of contributions come from South Africa. There is a slight improvement in US PGM share between 2024 and 2025, from 14% to 21%. From one viewpoint, that's a ~50% increase. However, it is still marginal compared to the South African share. As for gold—another core revenue driver—gold is 100% based in South Africa. There is no international component.

This explains why any jurisdictional risk overwhelmingly reflects on SBSW's valuation. If there is a discount—there is—it is not temporary but structural.

The Discount Isn't Caused by Operational Weakness

SBSW trades at a significant discount to its peers. On forward estimates, it trades at only ~3.5-3.7x earnings, where the sector median is ~16x. That's a 78% discount. On EV/EBITDA, there's again a ~73% discount: ~2.3x forward vs. ~8.4x sector. Even on sales metrics, SBSW trades at ~0.98x forward EV/Sales vs. ~1.88x, a ~48% discount.

This is not random. This is a deep and persistent discount across the entire valuation framework. It cannot be explained by operational constraints alone.

Purely operationally, SBSW is not a broken business. The miner generated ~$2.3B in EBITDA in 2025. Core segments, like the South African PGM and gold operations, are both profitable. SA PGM generated ~$1B, while gold contributed ~$750M in EBITDA. Even international mines like US PGMs and recycling are EBITDA-positive, contributing ~$260 million and ~$240 million, respectively.

SBSW's balance sheet is also strong, with an EV of ~$11.1B and net debt/EBITDA of 0.59x. It has exposure to critical minerals like platinum, palladium, and gold. None of these are structurally impaired assets.

These numbers suggest that SBSW's deep discount does not come from weak operations or lack of profitability. Rather, it points to one structural factor that cannot be easily mitigated and one that shapes how the entire business is perceived and evaluated. This is jurisdiction.

The South Africa Discount

South Africa ranks poorly as a mining jurisdiction: In its 2025 Annual Survey of Mining Companies, The Fraser Institute ranked South Africa 57th out of 68 jurisdictions on overall investment attractiveness, with a score of 52.97. The country was listed among the 10 least attractive jurisdictions globally on the Policy Perception Index, alongside countries such as Mali, Burkina Faso, the DRC, Angola, and Ecuador. This becomes important because, as Fraser notes, ~40% of mining investment decisions are driven by policy factors, not geology.

The operational backdrop remains constrained: Another resource, the SA Mine 2025 report from PwC, is more directly critical of the operating environment in South Africa. The report says there is persistent cost pressure, policy uncertainty, and infrastructure bottlenecks in the country. Power is unreliable, and mining companies face intermittent disruptions.

For SBSW, this is not theoretical. SBSW has contracted ~765 MW of private renewable capacity. It says it is now the largest private renewable energy offtaker in South African mining. This masks the fact that SBSW has been forced to take these measures just to ensure it can become free from South Africa's power problems. The company targets to meet ~56% of its energy demand from renewables by 2028. The worry, then, is for the other 44%.

Not just power, the PwC report says that logistics is another binding constraint, with Transnet's coal export line operating at only ~62% of capacity. This limits throughput despite favorable pricing.

Illegal mining and security are measurable financial risks: Further from the PwC report, South African government estimates show that there are ~6,000 abandoned mines and ~$3.6B in losses due to illegal mining. Sibanye itself reported 1,720 security incidents and ~$3.2 million in direct losses from copper theft in 2024—as cited by PwC.

Just recently, a Sibanye-related litigation attorney was shot and killed in what authorities described as a likely targeted attack. Her death created "fears from South Africa's business community," and has "intensified scrutiny around labor tensions in South Africa's mining sector, even as Sibanye Stillwater recently secured a three-year wage agreement with major unions."

South Africa's mining base is structurally difficult: PwC also notes that South Africa's gold output has declined. It used to be the world's largest producer in the 1990s—today it stands in tenth place. This has been caused by ageing mines as well as operational challenges like the ones we just highlighted. In the context of "future metals," PwC says that the country is not being adequately explored, and licensing processes are slow and non-transparent. South Africa has limited battery-grade processing capacity. There is also a skilled labor shortage in hydrometallurgy and chemical processing.

Diversification Efforts Are Real, But Not Enough

Management is aware of this situation and is clearly trying to diversify. They are building non-South African assets in recycling, US PGMs, lithium, nickel, zinc, copper, and other "green metals." They are expanding into the US, Finland, France, Australia, and Argentina.

However, the earnings base is still overwhelmingly South African. Their 100% gold component and near-80% PGM component come from that one geography. Indeed, South Africa is still a key destination for PGM, so it does not appear that SBSW will be able to divest out of the country quickly enough. For reference, their Keliber plant in Finland is going through a staged ramp-up because of lithium market volatility. Management has also hinted at internal organic growth rather than aggressive external expansion, which highlights the concern that SBSW will remain a majorly South African operation for the foreseeable future.

This is the key point. There may be marginal diversification, but not enough to impact the discount. SBSW may be an international miner, but that is very nominal. At its core, it is a South African mining business and comes with all the associated benefits and risks.

Valuation—What Needs to Change for a Re-Rating

In terms of its geographic earnings mix, SBSW, as we have seen, is overwhelmingly South African. The market is assigning one multiple for its South African earnings and a higher one for its non-South African (mostly US) earnings.

We can make an estimate about this by looking at miners like SBSW that are overwhelmingly South Africa-focused—names like Valterra Platinum Limited (ANGPY) and Impala Holdings (IMPUY) come to mind—versus a majorly US-focused miner like Newmont (NEM).

Newmont trades at ~13.1x forward earnings and ~6.8x forward EV/EBITDA. By contrast, Impala, a South Africa/Zimbabwe-heavy PGM miner, trades at only ~7.3x forward earnings and ~4.6x forward EV/EBITDA. Valterra Platinum, another South African PGM name, trades at ~6.4x forward EV/EBITDA and ~2.6x forward EV/Sales. While these are not directly comparable because of differences in mix and so on, one can see materially higher multiples for major US-based miners.

A simple framework makes clear what needs to change for SBSW. Let us assign a ~3.0-4.0x EV/EBITDA to South African operations and ~6.0-8.0x to non-South African operations to reflect the jurisdictional risk. Then, we can get a blended valuation table:

We can now see why SBSW trades at a steep discount to the ~8-9x sector median. Even if SBSW manages to expand meaningfully outside South Africa, the implied multiple only moves towards ~5x. This means that the discount comes from where earnings are generated.

If South Africa's share of EBITDA falls below ~50%, and non-South African operations scale materially, a re-rating may occur. Until then, despite strong operations and balance sheets, valuation will likely remain capped by inherent jurisdictional risk.

Conclusion—Cheap for a Reason, Not Yet for a Rerating

Sibanye Stillwater looks cheap on earnings, EBITDA, and sales not because it is being mispriced, but because the market is discounting its jurisdictional concentration. This is not merely about perception, although perception may play a role. As I have shown, major authoritative studies have highlighted the jurisdictional risks associated with mining in South Africa. Uncertainties around power, transport, licensing, labor, and other disruptions persist in South Africa, and the market is therefore unlikely to value SBSW's earnings on par with developed-market peers.

So either one bets on a high commodity upcycle that produces such strong earnings as to overwhelm the multiple issues or on a long-term geographic shift away from South Africa and other troubling geographies. Unless at least one of these happens, SBSW will continue to trade cheap. Given my focus on lower-risk assets, I will consider SBSW a sell/avoid.

data.title: Riskfield: The Growth Story Is Even Better Today
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on Riskfield Ltd. (RSKD) was a buy rating because I believed the business should see growth accelerate in the coming years given how well its platform strategy is scaling and that new products are seeing solid adoption trends. My view is even more positive now. The demand story looks cleaner, the platform strategy is contributing real revenue, and retention is improving at the same time. On top of that, agentic commerce could expand the number of problems RSKD can solve, which should support growth from here.

The growth outlook got better

The most important reason to continue staying bullish on RSKD is because the demand story now looks cleaner. As a recap, the growth story logic is still largely the same, in that RSKD is seeing solid pipeline strength and healthy renewal outcomes, and the platform should slowly contribute more to growth. We now have better proof that this logic is holding true and, in fact, has gotten stronger. You only need to look at RSKD's latest numbers to arrive at this conclusion. In the recent quarter (Q4), RSKD saw the highest quarterly amount of new business since the IPO, and that one quarter accounted for ~55% of all new business won in 2025; competitive win rates were also >75%, and lead generation at the top of the funnel went up ~50% y/y. These numbers tell me that RSKD growth momentum is not slowing down at all.

The platform strategy has also gotten more tangible, as I expected. We now have good data to better link new products and actual economics. Some of these new products, including Policy Protect, Account Secure, and Dispute Resolve, have already generated ~$10 million of annual revenue in 2025, and management is guiding for this set of products plus related non-guaranteed payment flows to reach ~$15 million to $20 million in 2026. Obviously, this is not enough to carry RSKD's entire growth story, but it is large enough to matter if the pace of growth is 50% to 100% a year. If RSKD makes $20 million revenue from these products this year, that itself is $10 million incremental revenue, or ~3% revenue growth contribution. Broadly, this also shows that RSKD is able to roll out new products and convert them into actual revenue.

Even if we look at RSKD's current customer base, the demand profile is getting better too, and we can infer this from the retention data. Both annual dollar retention [ADR] and net dollar retention [NDR] improved by 400 bps and 900 bps, respectively, and at the same time, the number of merchants using >1 product went up ~50% in FY2025. Put together, this suggests RSKD is not only winning customers, but it is also able to keep them and even expand wallet share, which makes it even more embedded in how clients operate.

Agentic commerce

The last point I would emphasize is agentic commerce. I believe the rise of agentic commerce is going to make the entire operating environment much more complex for merchants and, at the same time, expand RSKD's role in solving it. There are two types of agentic flows: (1) general-purpose LLMs that help consumers discover products and move closer to checkout and (2) merchant-native AI agents that sit inside the merchant ecosystem and manage more of the full shopping journey (including things like refunds, exchanges, etc.). As you can imagine, these really enhance the customers' experience and are something merchants would likely adopt.

However, this also creates new trust and fraud issues, and my view is signal loss is the main problem. According to management in the Q4 earnings call, when consumers transact through general-purpose LLMs, ~30% to 40% of key fraud signals can be lost. Flip it around, and it means that a merchant could see fraud rates go up by a decent amount, and that is a big deal. The idea is simple: fraud prevention gets harder when there is less visibility into the normal behavior that helps separate good users from bad ones. This is where RSKD expertise comes into play, since its response is not just about scoring the transaction. It is also helping merchants manage these lower-signal environments by segmenting order flows, spotting new fraud patterns faster, and giving merchants visibility they would not otherwise have.

I see a few ways this translates into revenue growth for RSKD. First, it can capture demand from existing merchants that are starting to test agentic channels and do not want to take higher fraud losses while they experiment. Second, since RSKD is no longer only about checkout protection, it can address a wider set of fraud and trust problems, including areas like returns, refunds, disputes, and account security.

That said, I do not think the right focus is that agentic commerce will suddenly drive a near-term jump in revenue. The framing should be that this increases the importance of RSKD's platform, and since RSKD already has the data, the identity layer, and the merchant relationships, it should be well positioned to capture demand here.

Valuation

My model still points to very attractive upside for RSKD's stock. What I have upgraded here is that growth acceleration should be faster. I now expect growth to accelerate to 10% in FY2026 (this is as guided), 15% in FY2027, and 20% in FY2028, breaking past the previous hurdle of mid-teens revenue growth. I don't think my growth assumptions are overly ambitious given the win rates, lead generation growth, and AI story. For multiples, I am still of the view that it should at least go back to 1.4x, but I remind readers that the broader cybersecurity players like Fortinet (FTNT) and Check Point Software (CHKP) trade at ~7x and 4x NTM revenue with high-single-digit to mid-teen revenue growth. Assuming a 1.4x multiple, I think the stock can easily re-rate back to ~$6.8 by the end of FY2027.

Risk

The revenue acceleration story still depends on timing. RSKD has won strong new business, but if the timing of merchant launches gets delayed, reported growth will not accelerate as fast as I expected. In addition, while the agentic commerce opportunity is interesting, it is still early. If that demand takes longer to convert into meaningful volume, the market may stay unconvinced that this newer growth driver can materially drive growth.

Conclusion

My view for RSKD is still a buy rating. I remain bullish because the core growth story has gotten better. New business momentum is strong, newer products are becoming meaningful enough to support growth, and retention trends suggest RSKD is getting more embedded with customers. I also think the agentic commerce angle matters, not because it will drive an immediate revenue jump, but because it can make RSKD's platform more relevant as fraud risks get more complex.
